title: Testing & Debugging
subtitle: Unit tests, integration tests, TDD, logging, and finding bugs before users do

sections.title: The Testing Pyramid
sections.text:
Tests give you confidence to deploy. The testing pyramid is your guide — write many small fast tests, fewer medium ones, and a few big slow ones.

  • Unit Tests — Test a single function or class in isolation. Mock everything else. Run in milliseconds. Should be your most numerous.
  • Integration Tests — Test how parts work together. Hit the real database, the real API. They're slower and harder to write, but catch things unit tests miss.
  • End-to-End (E2E) Tests — Test the entire system. Spin up the server, open a browser, simulate real user actions, verify the UI. Slow and brittle — use sparingly for critical paths (login, checkout).

Also: Contract tests (do APIs agree on the expected payload?) and Regression tests (did this fix break something that worked before?).

config.bottomLabel: E2E Tests (Few) — slow, brittle, critical-path only

sections.caption: The testing pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, a few E2E tests
sections.callout: Follow the 80/20 rule: 80% unit tests, 15% integration, 5% E2E. This gives you fast feedback AND confidence that your app works end-to-end.

sections.title: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
sections.text:
TDD is a discipline: Red → Green → Refactor.

  1. Red — Write a test for the feature you WISH existed. It fails (red) because you haven't built anything yet.
  2. Green — Write the minimum code to make the test pass. Not elegant. Just make it pass.
  3. Refactor — Clean up the code. Remove duplication. Make it elegant. Tests ensure you didn't break anything.

This guarantees 100% test coverage of what's important — every feature has a test because the test was written first. It also forces you to write testable code. If something is hard to test, it's probably badly designed.

TDD isn't always the right call. For prototypes or exploratory code, it slows you down. Use it for core business logic, complex algorithms, and anything that MUST be correct.
sections.caption: TDD cycle: Red (failing test) → Green (make it pass) → Refactor (clean up)

sections.title: Debugging Strategies
sections.text:
Bugs are inevitable. Good debugging is systematic, not random.

  • Reproduce it — Can you make it happen consistently? Write down the exact steps.
  • Read the error message — Stack traces tell you exactly where it crashed. Start there.
  • Divide and conquer — Binary search through the code. Is the bug before or after line 50? Check. Before or after line 25? Check. Halve the problem each time.
  • Log everything — console.log isn't elegant but it works. Better: structured logging with levels (info, warn, error) and request IDs so you can trace a single user's journey.
  • Rubber duck debugging — Explain the problem to an inanimate object. Out loud. Nine times out of ten you'll spot the bug yourself.

items.heading: Reproduce
items.body: Make it happen consistently. Write the exact steps.

items.heading: Read Stack Traces
items.body: The error tells you where to start looking.

items.heading: Binary Search
items.body: Halve the code each time. Is the bug before or after mid-point?

items.heading: Log + Trace IDs
items.body: Add request IDs to logs. Trace a request across services.

items.heading: Check Recent Changes
items.body: git diff HEAD~1 — what changed? That's likely the culprit.

items.heading: Rubber Duck
items.body: Explain out loud to a rubber duck. You'll see the bug.

sections.caption: Systematic debugging: don't guess, follow the process
sections.callout: git bisect is a superpower — it does binary search through your commit history to find exactly when a bug was introduced. Use it.
